step.title: Step 40
step.decisionPreview: print("Clicking on M square 5 more times to see the pattern...")

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 53 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what happened after ACTION7.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what happened after ACTION7
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"=== M squares ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M':
        print(f"  {n['boundary']}")

# Check white bar
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
white_count = ascii_lines[63].count('W')
print(f"\nWhite bar count: {white_count}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 moved the M square back! It was at (34-35, 15-16) and now it's at (35-36, 16-17). So ACTION7 is an undo action!
The white bar stayed at 42, which means ACTION7 doesn't cost a white bar tick.
So ACTION7 is undo. This means I can undo the clicks I made on the M squares.
But wait, I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about this more carefully.
The M squares move diagonally up-left when clicked. ACTION7 undoes the last action.
The goal might be to move the M squares to specific positions. But I don't know what those positions are.
Let me think about the level 1 mechanic again. In level 1, the gray square moved in a spiral pattern when clicked. The goal was to move the gray square to the yellow square.
In level 2, the M squares move diagonally up-left when clicked. The goal might be to move the M squares to the yellow square, but they can't reach it by moving diagonally up-left.
Wait, maybe the M squares change direction when they hit a boundary. Let me continue clicking on the M square to see if it changes direction when it hits the left or top boundary.
Actually, the M square at (35-36, 16-17) is moving up-left. If I keep clicking, it will eventually hit the top or left boundary. Let me see what happens then.
But I'm running out of actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is not to move the M squares to the yellow square. Maybe the goal is to move the M squares to specific positions that form a pattern.
Looking at the M square positions:
- (35-36, 16-17)
- (39-40, 36-37)
- (55-56, 46-47)
- (56-57, 15-16)
These positions form a rough rectangle. Maybe the goal is to move them to form a different pattern.
Or maybe the goal is to move the M squares to the blue shape's position. The blue shape is at (23-31, 29-37).
Actually, let me think about this differently. The M squares move diagonally up-left when clicked. This means their r - c value is constant. Let me check the r - c values again:
- M square at (35-36, 16-17): r - c = 35 - 16 = 19
- M square at (39-40, 36-37): r - c = 39 - 36 = 3
- M square at (55-56, 46-47): r - c = 55 - 46 = 9
- M square at (56-57, 15-16): r - c = 56 - 15 = 41
The blue shape is at (23-31, 29-37). The center is around (27, 33). The r - c value is 27 - 33 = -6.
None of the M squares have r - c = -6, so they can't reach the blue shape's center by moving diagonally up-left.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the M squares don't move diagonally up-left. Maybe they move in a spiral pattern, but I haven't clicked enough times to see the full pattern.
Let me click on the M square several more times to see if it changes direction.
Actually, I already clicked on the M square twice, and it moved up-left both times. Let me click on it a few more times to see if it changes direction.
But I need to be efficient. Let me click on the M square 5 more times and see the pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me click on the M square several more times to see if it changes direction
# Current position: (35-36, 16-17)
print("Clicking on M square 5 more times to see the pattern...")
actions = []
for i in range(5):
    actions.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35 - i, 'col': 16 - i})
action(actions)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content: Clicking on M square 5 more times to see the pattern...
